theme.name: Are We There Yet?
theme.summary: The 2026 theme explores travel, waiting, progress, thresholds, drafts, roads, navigation, and futures that always seem almost here.

insights.title: Destination vs. journey
insights.body: The theme keeps asking whether we should value arrival, motion itself, or the moment of transition between the two.

insights.title: Progress vs. waiting
insights.body: Progress bars, endless construction, adolescence, and political transitions all return to the same question: how much longer?

insights.title: Draft vs. final form
insights.body: Works of art, products, and even societies move through prototypes, revisions, and unfinished states before looking complete.

insights.title: Infrastructure vs. imagination
insights.body: Roads, hotels, sidewalks, navigation tools, and future predictions show how an idea becomes a livable world.

insights.title: Patience vs. quitting
insights.body: The ending of the theme asks its hardest question: when should we keep going, and when should we stop?

subjects.label: Special Area
subjects.description: Big ideas, the psychology of waiting, thresholds, mental prototypes, and the philosophy of getting there.

subjects.label: History
subjects.description: Roads, hotels, term limits, giant projects, navigation, and long traditions of movement.

subjects.label: Social Studies
subjects.description: Governments in transition, urban planning, tourism, collective behavior, and public policy.

subjects.label: Science & Technology
subjects.description: Navigation, cognition, adolescence, interfaces, AI, quantum computing, prototypes, and transport.

subjects.label: Art & Music
subjects.description: Paintings, songs, road imagery, creative drafts, and liminal landscapes.

subjects.label: Literature & Media
subjects.description: Travel narratives, ambiguous endings, storyboards, long takes, famous lines, and screen culture.

sections.title: Introductory Questions
sections.blurb: Beginnings, arrivals, points of no return, and what endings really mean.
sections.angle: The theme opens with its simplest and deepest question: what does it actually mean to be there?

sections.title: Progress, Not Regress
sections.blurb: Progress bars, time perception, and the management of waiting.
sections.angle: WSC asks how we measure progress and how we survive periods that feel motionless.

sections.title: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed
sections.originalTitle: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed
sections.blurb: To-do lists, unfinished memory, and ways of deciding what matters most.
sections.angle: Here, progress is measured as much by what we never finish as by what we do.

sections.title: The End is Nearish
sections.blurb: Political transitions, term limits, exits from office, and temporary in-between phases.
sections.angle: Societies also experience endings that are delayed, imperfect, or only partly complete.

sections.title: There's a Draft in Here
sections.blurb: Versions, sketches, rewrites, and the slow growth of finished work.
sections.angle: Before a final work appears, there is almost always a draft, and often several.

sections.title: We Are All in This to Get There
sections.originalTitle: We're All in This to Get There
sections.blurb: Megaprojects, delays, collective coordination, and construction that never seems to end.
sections.angle: The journey is not only personal. It is political, logistical, and collective.

sections.title: Where the Sidewalk Starts
sections.blurb: Sidewalks, moving walkways, stroads, and the spaces between different uses.
sections.angle: WSC pays attention to the modest surfaces that quietly make movement possible.

sections.title: Monkey See, Monkey Prototype
sections.blurb: Prototyping, testing, fidelity, wireframes, and biomimicry.
sections.angle: The future never arrives all at once. It is built through repeated experiments.

sections.title: The Lovely and the Liminal
sections.blurb: Thresholds, in-between spaces, the doorway effect, and the aesthetics of transition.
sections.angle: Being between two places, ages, or states becomes a major subject in its own right.

sections.title: Going Pains
sections.blurb: Adolescence, rites of passage, the developing brain, and in-between identities.
sections.angle: Human growth is a near-perfect example of being not there yet.

sections.title: Home and Wandering
sections.blurb: Navigation, migration, compasses, maps, and the pull of home.
sections.angle: To arrive anywhere, you first need some way to know where you are going.

sections.title: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
sections.originalTitle: Where We're Going, We'll Still Need Them
sections.blurb: Road networks, future infrastructure, routes, detours, and the road-trip imagination.
sections.angle: Even in the future, physical and symbolic roads shape how we move through the world.

sections.title: Call of Duty-Free
sections.blurb: Hotels, the Grand Tour, mass tourism, entrepots, and the movement of goods.
sections.angle: Travel is also about sleeping, trading, consuming, and leaving traces behind.

sections.title: Next Year in Futurism
sections.blurb: Perpetually promised technologies, AGI, quantum computing, fusion, and delayed futures.
sections.angle: The theme asks why some futures always seem to be just two years away.

sections.title: Concluding Questions
sections.blurb: Patience, quitting, Zeno's paradox, open endings, and what comes after arrival.
sections.angle: The theme closes by asking when to continue, when to stop, and where to go next.

questions.prompt: The saying 'A journey of a thousand miles begins with...' ends how?
questions.explanation: The line is traditionally associated with Laozi. It captures the idea that a vast journey begins with one very small decision.

questions.prompt: Which Lord of the Rings film is often remembered for having several fake endings?
options: The Hobbit: The Battle of the Five Armies
questions.explanation: The Return of the King is famous for stacking one ending after another, which fits the WSC question of endings that seem unwilling to end.

questions.prompt: Who sings 'And now, the end is near' at the start of 'My Way'?
questions.explanation: The song has become a classic emblem of reflection, closure, and theatrical endings.

questions.prompt: In Roman history, to 'cross the Rubicon' mainly means to...
options: pass a point of no return
questions.explanation: The phrase comes from Julius Caesar and now refers to making an irreversible choice.

questions.prompt: What kind of interface is used when progress is happening but the exact duration is unknown?
questions.explanation: When a percentage cannot be estimated reliably, designers often use an indeterminate indicator to show that work is still happening.

questions.prompt: In interface design, what is a skeleton screen?
options: a ghost layout shown before real content loads
a screen used only for security testing
questions.explanation: A skeleton screen displays placeholder blocks so that waiting feels shorter and more structured.

questions.prompt: In project management, a milestone is mainly...
questions.explanation: A milestone marks an important stage in the journey, much like a marker on a road.

questions.prompt: Which law says that work expands to fill the time available for its completion?
questions.explanation: Parkinson's law helps explain why progress can look slower simply because the time frame stretches.

questions.prompt: The Zeigarnik effect refers to the fact that we tend to remember...
options: unfinished tasks better than finished ones
questions.explanation: Unfinished tasks stay more active in the mind, which is why incompletion can feel so mentally noisy.

questions.prompt: The Eisenhower Matrix sorts tasks according to which two variables?
questions.explanation: The method contrasts urgency with importance to help people decide what actually deserves attention.

questions.prompt: Who popularized the note-taking and planning system known as the Bullet Journal?
questions.explanation: Ryder Carroll designed the method as a flexible way to organize tasks, notes, and reflection in one place.

questions.prompt: In the 'Eat That Frog' method, what should you do first?
options: the hardest or most important task
questions.explanation: The idea is to tackle the heaviest task first so that the unfinished item does not dominate the whole day.

questions.prompt: In many parliamentary systems, a caretaker government mainly exists to...
options: handle the transition around an election
questions.explanation: A caretaker government keeps things running during a transition without launching major new changes.

questions.prompt: Which amendment to the U.S. Constitution limits a president to two elected terms?
questions.explanation: The 22nd Amendment, adopted after Franklin Roosevelt, created the modern two-term limit.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'lame duck' usually refers to...
options: an outgoing officeholder with reduced influence
a leader without an absolute majority
questions.explanation: A lame duck is still in office but already on the way out, and therefore usually weaker politically.

questions.prompt: Why is the Roman figure Cincinnatus so often cited as a political model?
options: he gave up power and returned to his farm
he founded the first modern senate
questions.explanation: Cincinnatus symbolizes temporary power willingly surrendered, which makes him useful in discussions of endings in office.

questions.prompt: The working title 'Scrambled Eggs' eventually became which Beatles classic?
questions.explanation: Before reaching its final form, 'Yesterday' circulated under that temporary title, a perfect example of a masterpiece passing through draft form.

questions.prompt: Which Edvard Munch painting is often presented as the development of 'Despair'?
questions.explanation: The Scream intensifies and transforms the emotional world already present in Despair.

questions.prompt: In film and animation, what is a storyboard mainly used for?
questions.explanation: A storyboard turns an idea into a visible sequence before the camera or animation process begins.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a low-fidelity wireframe?
options: to test structure and flow quickly
questions.explanation: A low-fidelity wireframe helps teams test layout and user flow before investing in visual detail.

questions.prompt: In which city is the Sagrada Familia located?
questions.explanation: The Sagrada Familia in Barcelona has become one of the world's most famous symbols of the giant, unfinished project.

questions.prompt: What does Brooks's law suggest about a software project that is already late?
options: adding more people often makes it later
software delays do not really exist
questions.explanation: Brooks emphasized coordination costs: adding more people does not automatically speed up a late project.

questions.prompt: The Ryugyong Hotel, often cited as a famously delayed megaproject, is in...
questions.explanation: The North Korean Ryugyong Hotel is frequently mentioned in discussions of monumental, delayed, or stalled development.

questions.prompt: Berlin Brandenburg Airport became famous because it...
options: opened only after years of delays
questions.explanation: Its extended delay made it a modern case study in governance, planning, and project management.

questions.prompt: What is another common name for a moving walkway?
questions.explanation: Travelator is a common term for the moving walkway that helps pedestrians move forward.

questions.prompt: Why do many urbanist critics dislike the stroad?
options: it mixes the functions of a street and a fast road
it is reserved for historic centers
it exists only for heavy trucks
questions.explanation: A stroad combines the drawbacks of a street and a road: it is often unsafe, confusing, and unpleasant.

questions.prompt: Which ancient city is often cited for its raised stepping stones and early sidewalk-like features?
questions.explanation: Pompeii offers striking examples of organized pedestrian movement through raised stones and street design.

questions.prompt: In The Jetsons, moving sidewalks mainly symbolize...
options: a futuristic vision of automated convenience
questions.explanation: The Jetsons use mechanized infrastructure to imagine a future with less friction and more effortless movement.

questions.prompt: The main purpose of a prototype is usually to...
options: freeze the design after the first version
questions.explanation: A prototype exists to teach, test, and refine before large-scale production begins.

questions.prompt: Which option best represents high fidelity?
options: an interactive prototype close to the final product
questions.explanation: The higher the fidelity, the more closely the prototype resembles the eventual product.

questions.prompt: To preserve secrecy, some early iPhone prototypes were hidden inside what?
questions.explanation: Apple reportedly disguised some prototypes inside hardware that looked more like iPods than phones.

questions.prompt: Biomimicry means...
options: drawing design inspiration from living systems
questions.explanation: Biomimicry studies strategies from the living world and adapts them into human designs and systems.

questions.prompt: The doorway effect mainly refers to the tendency to...
options: forget why you entered a room
change your accent in a new place
questions.explanation: Crossing from one space into another can disrupt the short-term maintenance of an intention.

questions.prompt: The word 'liminal' comes from a Latin word meaning...
questions.explanation: It comes from limen, the Latin word for threshold.

questions.prompt: Which film was actually shot in a single continuous take inside the Hermitage Museum?
questions.explanation: Russian Ark is famous for being filmed in one continuous shot, making it a powerful example of cinematic passage through space.

questions.prompt: Which Edward Hopper painting is often discussed as an icon of suspended, liminal urban space?
questions.explanation: Nighthawks captures a feeling of being between inside and outside, contact and isolation.

questions.prompt: The prefrontal cortex generally continues maturing until about...
questions.explanation: Its slow development helps explain why adolescence remains an extended transitional state.

questions.prompt: The modern social category of the 'teenager' became especially prominent in which century?
questions.explanation: The idea of the teenager rose sharply in the 20th century alongside mass schooling, youth culture, and targeted consumption.

questions.prompt: The term neophilia refers to...
questions.explanation: Neophilia is a tendency to seek or enjoy novelty, often linked to exploration and risk-taking.

questions.prompt: Which anthropologist is especially associated with the concept of rites of passage?
questions.explanation: Arnold van Gennep developed the classic framework of separation, liminality, and reintegration.

questions.prompt: The magnetic compass was first developed in...
questions.explanation: China played a major role in the development of the compass, which transformed long-distance navigation.

questions.prompt: What does a sextant mainly measure?
options: the angle between a celestial object and the horizon
questions.explanation: A sextant helps estimate position by measuring the angle of a celestial body relative to the horizon.

questions.prompt: North American monarch butterflies notably migrate toward...
questions.explanation: Their migration to Mexico is one of the best-known examples of extraordinary animal navigation.

questions.prompt: GPS mainly locates a device through...
questions.explanation: The system compares signal travel times from multiple satellites to estimate position.

questions.prompt: John Loudon McAdam is associated with what kind of road surface?
questions.explanation: Macadam became one of the defining technologies in the history of modern road building.

questions.prompt: What does Braess's paradox show about traffic systems?
options: a new road can sometimes make traffic worse
roundabouts are always better than traffic lights
questions.explanation: The paradox shows that a richer network is not always more efficient when many people optimize for themselves at once.

questions.prompt: What nickname helped make Route 66 famous in the American imagination?
questions.explanation: Route 66 is widely known as 'The Mother Road,' a title reinforced by literature, music, and road-trip mythology.

questions.prompt: Which great ancient route linked Susa and Sardis in the Persian Empire?
questions.explanation: The Persian Royal Road allowed messages, resources, and authority to move quickly across the empire.

questions.prompt: The Grand Tour was mainly undertaken by...
questions.explanation: The Grand Tour functioned as an educational and cultural rite of passage for elite Europeans.

questions.prompt: Which Japanese establishment is often described as the oldest operating hotel in the world?
questions.explanation: Nishiyama Onsen Keiunkan frequently appears in discussions of the oldest hotels still in operation.

questions.prompt: An entrepot mainly serves to...
options: store and redistribute goods in transit
questions.explanation: An entrepot works as a node for storage, exchange, and re-export rather than final local use.

questions.prompt: Dark tourism refers to visits to...
options: sites associated with death, disaster, or trauma
questions.explanation: Dark tourism raises difficult questions about memory, curiosity, respect, and the consumption of tragedy.

questions.prompt: Nuclear fusion naturally powers...
questions.explanation: The Sun runs on fusion, which is why scientists hope to reproduce the process as a human energy source.

questions.prompt: Nikola Tesla's Wardenclyffe Tower was mainly intended to...
options: transmit wireless communications and possibly power
questions.explanation: Wardenclyffe represented Tesla's ambitious vision of global wireless transmission, but it never fully came to life.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'quantum supremacy' refers to the moment when...
options: a quantum computer performs a task impractical for a classical one
quantum physics replaces all classical physics
classical processors vanish from the market
questions.explanation: The term refers to superiority on a specific computational task, not the total replacement of classical computing.

questions.prompt: AGI stands for...
questions.explanation: AGI refers to the idea of an artificial intelligence able to learn and reason across a wide range of contexts.

questions.prompt: The sunk cost fallacy is the tendency to...
options: continue mainly because you already invested heavily
questions.explanation: This bias pushes people to keep going because they have already paid, even when quitting would be wiser.

questions.prompt: In modern mathematics, Zeno's dichotomy paradox is partly resolved by the idea that...
options: an infinite number of steps can converge to a finite total
questions.explanation: Convergent series show how infinitely many divisions can still produce a finite overall distance.

questions.prompt: What was Francis Fukuyama mainly arguing with the phrase 'The End of History'?
options: that no more events would happen
that ancient history no longer mattered
that liberal democracy seemed to have triumphed as an ideological horizon
that all monarchies would disappear immediately
questions.explanation: Fukuyama was not claiming that nothing would happen, but that one political model seemed to have won the great ideological contest of the era.

questions.prompt: Which television series is famous for ending with a cut to black?
questions.explanation: The ending of The Sopranos became one of the best-known examples of a deliberately ambiguous conclusion.
